edge: YOCO is a decoder-decoder architecture designed for large language models.
YOCO architecture only caches key-value pairs once for large language models.
YOCO consists of a cross-decoder component stacked upon a self-decoder.
YOCO includes a self-decoder that efficiently encodes global key-value caches.
KV caches are reused by the cross-decoder via cross-attention in YOCO architecture.
YOCO model behaves like a decoder-only Transformer, although YOCO only caches once.
YOCO design substantially reduces GPU memory demands while retaining global attention capability.
The computation flow in YOCO enables prefilling to early exit without changing the final output, significantly speeding up the prefill stage.
YOCO achieves favorable performance compared to Transformer in various settings of scaling up model size and number of training tokens.
YOCO is extended to 1M context length with near-perfect needle retrieval accuracy.
YOCO improves inference memory, prefill latency, and throughput by orders of magnitude across context lengths and model sizes.
The decoder-only architecture is established as the standard option.

SwiGLU is defined as a function that involves swish, X, weights W1, and W2. It is used in relation to efficient self-attention in the given context.
RMSNorm is used for LN(·), and LN is used in efficient self-attention, establishing a relationship between the two terms.
Both efficient self-attention and causal masking are used together, indicating a functional relationship between them.
KV caches generated by self-decoder module are used by cross-decoder in the context, creating a connection between efficient self-attention and cross-decoder.
The output of self-decoder generates KV caches for cross-decoder, implying an association between the two modules.
Attention function is applied in cross-decoder modules to calculate Yl, indicating a dependency between cross-decoder and Attention.
Causal masking is also used for cross-attention in cross-decoder, establishing a link between the two modules.
YOCO significantly reduces serving costs and improves inference performance compared to Transformer. YOCO roughly saves L times GPU memory for caches compared to Transformer.
YOCO reduces serving costs and improves inference performance by reusing global KV caches and requiring about O(N) caches compared to N x L keys and values in Transformer decoders.
YOCO enables serving more tokens by having a much smaller amount of CL caches compared to N x L keys and values in Transformer decoders.
YOCO enables to serve many more tokens without being out of GPU memory by saving GPU memory for caches compared to Transformer.
The increased batch size is beneficial to inference throughput, which can lead to being out of GPU memory.
The cross-decoder reuses the outputs of the self-decoder, leading to early exit before entering the cross-decoder during the prefill stage.
The intriguing property of computation dependency greatly accelerates the prefilling speed.
Only half the layers are needed for forward computation, resulting in at least half prefilling latency reduction.
The efficient attention modules of the self-decoder contribute to reducing the prefilling latency.
For the example of 512K context length, the prefilling latency can be decreased to less than 6 seconds, showcasing a significant reduction.
YOCO presents about three times speedup in terms of prefilling time compared to the example of 32K length.

Various efficient self-attention methods can be chosen for the self-decoder, impacting the cache memory complexity and deployment costs.
Sliding-window attention restricts the attention range into a fixed window size C, unlike vanilla Transformer decoders which attend to all previous tokens.
During inference, the KV cache memory complexity can be reduced from O(N) to O(C) by sliding-window attention, making memory usage constant instead of increasing with sequence length.
Similar to multi-head self-attention, sliding-window attention computes the output via a specific formula, allowing for effective computation.
Gated retention augments retention with a data-dependent gating mechanism, enhancing the efficiency of the self-attention module.
Gated retention unifies parallel, recurrent, and chunkwise recurrent computation paradigms, achieving the same computation results through different computation methods.
Gated retention combines parallel, recurrent, and chunkwise recurrent computation paradigms to achieve efficient computation and data processing.
